speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. Magda, we left off on the first tape with you discussing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the rampant anti-Semitism that you felt and experienced in the schools. What other restrictions occurred at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this time, there were more and more restrictions in the school. And also the restrictions, as far as my father was concerned, his job. He couldn't be, anymore, a chief accountant. He couldn't be, anymore, even an accountant in that-- in the-- in the-- the position that he held before. He couldn't maintain. And then he had to take the three jobs, which meant poorly-paid jobs. My poor father was working, many times, half of the night, in order that, you know, we should-- we should exist. Then because of that, you had to go into smaller apartments, apartment houses. And then the restrictions got worse and worse. Jewish people were not allowed to have help. And we had somebody who was helping, was washing the laundry, was working for my grandmother, you know, my grandparents. My grandfather was a businessman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so they-- they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this-- this was getting really bad. You know? And although we had this-- this woman who worked for my grandparents, and he-- she said, well, she doesn't care, if she's punished. Because she worked for this family for years. But you know, there were more and more restrictions. And in the schools, they tried to lower you a grade. If you had good grades, for instance, in the major subjects, and you-- you-- you worked hard for it, then-- for instance, I was very good in gymnastics. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did fencing. I was in competitive fencing when I was a teenager. I was fencing with-- you know, my uncle was Romanian fencing champion.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eugene-- it's Yeno, you know, Hungarian name, Yeno Moses. And I was really good in gymnastics. That they tried to lower your grade in gymnastics or in arts so that you-- that's a persecution, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- that you couldn't complain [? what, about ?] injustices done in school. You couldn't complain. Because if my parents would go and complain, they would persecute me more. Or maybe I would-- they would just eliminate me. So you had to-- as time went on, when I was in-- in the fifth gymnasium, you had sixth gymnasium. But you see, when it come to 1940, things changed dramatically, for the worse, for the worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in the-- in the-- already, you know, the-- the Hungarians were totally-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1940, you know, there were Hungary, suddenly change hands. I mention, to you, that.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened, you know, Hitler took power. He occupied Czechoslovakia and Poland in 1939. You know? So in 1940, he's-- that's when it dramatically changed. So you know how we speak about '30s, when it started, gradual. But in 1940, it hit us hard, harder than ever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Hitler promised Hungary northern part of Transylvania, if they join. And they-- they joined Hitler. They also participated in the Russian Offensive in 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then the government was very anti-Semitic. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --we-- we had a-- a worse times. We had to keep in radios. They were talking about Jewish people had to give in radios. Jewish people maybe have to give in their jewelry.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were starting to rob you. You know, not only that they took away, already, your jobs. But they-- they tried to rob you. Not even-- that-- that you couldn't even maintain the-- the living standard that you had before.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they gradually, you know, gradually, gradually. This so-called partial liberty which we enjoyed, you know? We enjoyed partial liberty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because although we were citizens, we didn't have all the rights. You know, we were deprived of our rights. And then this partial liberty, naturally, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] turned into total loss of liberty when Hitler occupied it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the 1940s, before Hitler came, there were very, very hard times already, for us Jewish people. And they were gradually threatening to take away the-- you know, the property of my grandfather. My grandfather had started, you know? Everything he had, it was his own work. You know? And he was-- he had-- he became a very well-to-do man. You know? He had estates. He had an alcohol factory. He had a mill. You know? And his two sons were in those-- in those estates, farms. And they were talking about taking away the farms. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a terrible thing, because even before Hitler came in, you know, they came down and they chased away my-- his sons, you know, his two sons. They said that they were able to shoot him. They have to leave the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? We were robbed gradually. That's what happened. More and more and more. So for us Jewish people, when Hitler came to power, it was doomsday for us. Because we were affected. Now, the Jewish people were affected in Romania, too. There was anti-Semitism, you know? Although 1940, southern part of Transylvania was Romania. But look what happened to my husband. You know? They-- they didn't allow Jewish people to go-- to be in medical school. You know? I mean, it was-- it was terrible. And so we had a-- a kind of a-- an introduction into a big persecution before Hitler occupied our part-- the northern part of Transylvania. See, that's what's happened. So Hitler-- Hitler came-- why-- but why did they occupy northern part of Transylvania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was in 1944, and Hungary was already seeing that, you know, it's not a good thing to be with Hitler anymore. So they wanted to-- to make a separate peace treaty with the Allies. And then Hitler, naturally, occupied it. And then we were punished by this very badly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the day that the Germans marched in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember the day and every single event. I can never forget it. They-- they came-- they occupied the city of Cluj, in 1944. It was March, 19th. And naturally, we Jewish people were very afraid. We did not know what's happening in Poland, or-- or in the countries which he occupied. Because I think if we would have known, we would have done something about it. But we were ignorant. And my father, you know, who was growing up in the Austro-Hungarian Empire, as it were, you have German, you have Beethovens, and you have Mozarts, and culture. And-- and he knew the Germans from before.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these were not the same, you see? And he said, well, there's nothing drastic might happen. That's what we-- that's what we believed. This is why we were sitting ducks to be taken to the camps. You know? And-- so it was, for us-- I never forget that day. Because shortly after that date, they-- they came in-- they occupied the city on March 19th.

speaker: interviewee
text: And already, everything was, now, going fast, fast. It was April, 1944. And in April, already, all Jewish people were forced of-- to-- to wear, at all times, if you go out on the streets, the Star of David. Which we had to cut out. It's a kind of a yellow canvas-type material, and pin it to our coats. And you know, the city of Cluj was surrounded by the Carpathian Mountains and the Transylvanian Alps. So end of March, there, it was-- in April, it was still cool. So you had to go with your coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you could have been stopped on the street. You know? On our identification card was written the religion. So you were stamped, Jew. And if you would-- if they would find you, that you are a Jew, and you don't wear your yellow star, they could put you in prison. Or who knows what they could do to you? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought that it was very humiliating that we had to walk the streets, so everyone could see, from far, the stamp of our religion. And we-- our fathers were, like, treated like cancerous growths, like infectious disease, you know, that people have to stay away from us. Naturally, also, it was dangerous. Because you could have been molested. From that time on, my father didn't want me to go alone on the streets. And we were afraid to go out. Yet you had to go out. Because you had to get your supplies. You-- you could not afford-- allow yourself not to go out. And then things went really rapidly, you know, from there on. It was March. April, wear the Star of David. May, May was a month I will never forget. Because in May, they were pulling us out of our homes. Their special Hungarian police with the SS, German soldiers, were going from home to home, taking the Jews out of their house. At the city hall, they had the list of the Jewish peoples and their addresses. So they went from street to street. And you know, I was so young at that time. I was 18 years old. And I felt that something terrible is going to come. And I said to my parents, we have to do something about it. I don't want to go, with the Star of David, you know, on the streets. And my-- my parents sometimes had a hard time with me. Because I would not put on. I would rip off my star and walk on the street like a free person, for-- at least, even, for half hour. But you know, now thinking back, it was terrible that we were not informed. We had to give our radios in. So we had no radio. That was punished if you were-- had the radios. Already, you know, the radios had to be taken in. So that you are in complete darkness.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't know what is coming. So-- and-- and I'm just surprised, you know? We could-- we could see the-- the-- the police going from house to house. And we could see how people are treated. Why didn't we do anything about it? I can't understand even now, why? Because it was-- you know why? Because it was too late. If somebody wanted to go into the hiding, should have gone earlier. And we had some relatives in southern part of Romania. You know? In southern part of Transylvania, which was Romania. All what we had to do is just go maybe 60 miles, 80 miles, and get out of there. But-- but-- but then the Jews from the city were taken out. Now, what happened? I always thought that maybe a miracle is going to happen, and they-- they will miss our house. But miracles like that don't happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, you know, I could hear the sound of the heavy boots. And the Hungarian police had some little bells, you know, attached to their boots. So I could hear the sound of the bells. At that time, we were already in an apartment house on the second floor. And they came into our house. And for the first time, I really experienced-- you know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --rudeness. I experienced something-- I grew up in a house, you know, when my father was a peace-loving man. He always say-- said to me, follow the broad, countless streets of knowledge. Beware the dark, narrow alleys of ignorance. Practice the art of love, forgiveness, and tolerance. I experienced lots of-- there was lots of love. We were a big family. And it was a loving family. And for the first time, you know, these strange people come in. They push you around. You had to take a-- a small suitcase or a few changes from-- you know, a few articles from the drawers, throw it in. And then they push you out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just a small suitcase with-- with a-- a few change, like underwear. And maybe, I don't even remember if we had time to take a dress. We went with-- with whatever we had on. And we had a coat. You know? And my father loved books. And we had a library, and a bookcase with a glass cover. He-- he always cherished books. He-- he said, books are something that give knowledge. Also loved music. He played three instruments. That's why I'm-- I'm writing music, and I-- I love music. And all my life, I grew up with music.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was something in that cabinet. And now I get very emotional about this. Because I loved writing. You know? Ever since I was very young.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I was a young teenager, I wrote short stories. And I had my little book of short stories locked in there. And that was, for me, an important thing. Because it was part of myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, my thoughts were, well, if I have to give up everything, all the material things I have, what's the most precious thing for me to take it along-- to take along? And I said, it's my little book. That's all I want.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took out the book. And I was holding it, you know, to my breast. It's just like an old friend. And then this police, the Hungarian police, you know, they-- they were just as bad as SS. They were special police force. He took it out of my hand, ripped it out of my hand. Not only took it out, but ripped it into pieces.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I could see one page of my book there. You know what it was? It was like somebody killed something, like part of me was killed on-- on that day. And then a-- a thought occurred. You know? I thought, they can take away my book. They cannot take away my thoughts and my mind. And I'm going to have, one day, my own book. And now, this is-- I have my four books published. You know? And two of them are dedicated for the Holocaust. Because the Holocaust is my lifetime mission. I am in public speaking for 22 years. I wrote the requiem, a musical-- a musical-- music for the-- for the Holocaust. And for-- it's composed for baritone solo, soprano solo, piano accompaniment, and the choral arrangement. I would like you to-- to listen to my music when we finish this. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: My heart goes out for my family who were killed in the camps. My heart goes out for all the family members I lost, which was 80% of my family. It's good that we were a big family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I still have some family members who are survivors. And we went-- nobody lives, anymore, in Transylvania, in the city of Cluj. I had-- my family was concentrated in that city. This is why the big loss I have, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but this was a horrible experience for me, that is going to stay, always, with me. The pain, the emotional pain. See, if they would have hit me, it wouldn't have been as bad. Because this was an emotional pain. Something was torn out of me. A part of me was killed. And then, you know, they-- they pushed us out from the house. Everything was looted. My poor mother had beautiful crafts work, photos. I had-- I never knew my great grandmother. It was a beautiful picture, a sketch done by a-- a hunchback, who was a great artist.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? They didn't-- didn't realize. And I loved that picture. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't take out anything with us. So the house was, after that, you know, everyone could go in and take-- pick what they wanted. Then we were put on-- on open trucks. The open trucks were waiting, you know, in the front of the house. My poor father was on a bicycle. And you know, he's always tried to get-- earn a few things. So he worked for a friend who-- who-- it wasn't allowed. But he wanted to help my father. He came with his bicycle. Now, this is also in my mind. He came with his bicycle. And they pushed us out, me and my mother. And my father wasn't home yet. And I pleaded with the-- with that policeman to help, wait until my father comes. You know, that was ridiculous. And it's God's way. My father just arrived when we were up on the-- on the-- on this open truck. He threw his bicycle and jumped up on the truck. Nothing. He took nothing with him. He came in what he was. And then we were taken-- because that was important, that we should be together, the family. So then we-- we were taken to the outskirts of the city, at the site of an old, abandoned factory. And these were called the ghettos. And we were-- naturally had the barbed wire. And we were guarded by the-- by guards, you know, by police, Hungarian police, and Germans. And it was terrible. There were terrible conditions. We had to sleep on the ground. We had-- we didn't bring any food. I mean, it was-- it was inhuman, the way, you know, we were treated there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember one incident. You know, there were latrines. You know? The camps started in the ghettos. I tell you something. There were latrines. And there was an old lady who really had to go. You know? And we had to stay. Because they numbered us. And-- and she asked the guard, I have to go. He said, make it in your pants. You know? I mean, I never can forget that. That poor woman. He said, do it in your pants. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- we-- we-- the ghettos-- they-- and they lied to us. They were great liars. First of all, they said that we are going to stay, [? provisorically, ?] in the ghettos. And then we are going to be taken, families, into forced-- into labor camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: But families are going to get-- to get-- be together. It's going to be much better. And we believed it. But I was suspicious. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I wanted to escape from the camps. They had to guard me, just like, you know, all eyes on me. Because I said to my father, said to my family, let's get away from here. Escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is not good. I don't like that whole thing. You know? It's nothing good. I didn't trust them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so sure enough, after a month being in the ghettos-- that was through May-- they started loading people for the-- in the cattle wagons. But we didn't know. See, they were taking transports.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, there was a Jewish committee. And they had to have a certain quota. You know? They had to have a certain quota every day, that they take away from there. You know? I don't know if they were informed where they go. You know? But they had to put up a quota of maybe 300 people, and 200 people, and 500 people. You know? And they were taken away. We didn't know what happens to them. Why they were taken away to Auschwitz. You know? And finally, our turn came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it was-- it was-- my father said, well, I hope we can drag it out so maybe we are the last ones. Maybe we can do something. But it was too late. It was too late. When you are guarded like that, you can be shot. I mean, this is no more time. And finally, one day, the last-- we were among the last transports from the ghetto. And then we were marching to the cattle wagons, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just stop you for a minute to ask you a little bit more about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a particular name, first of all, the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't think it had a particular name. It was just a Jewish ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did you work at all? Did they have you work inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We were kept, there, totally idle. Totally idle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think that-- that-- I know of two people who managed to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any kind of resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, there was a hospital there. And that was Professor Hainal. I never forget him. He-- we heard that he was hiding some Jews. Actually, when I came back from the camps, our neighbor-- you know, we had the small apartment-- they were hidden by Dr. Hainal, in the clinic, in the psychiatric ward.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were a few people who would really get, you know, risk to hide. But we didn't know where to hide, who-- whom to find. Was there any type of resistance movement that tried to being?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghettos?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Not to my knowledge. Now, I don't know. You know? But I am not aware that there was any resistance, not like in the Warsaw ghetto. You know? It wasn't the similar thing. You know? And then, you know, after a month, we were taken away from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any punishments at all while you were inside the ghetto? Did you see anybody being beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were treated like dirt. That's what I have to tell you. I did not witness actual beatings in the ghetto, no. But very vulgar and rude, and yelling at us. You know? We were-- I mean, I wasn't used to this kind of treatment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you-- did you have a room that they put you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We slept on the ground. And I think we had some tents.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in our tent, I was-- oh well, there were several thousand. There were-- there were the Jews from Cluj, with us. There were the Jews from Cluj. It was pretty big, the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Jewish committee? Do you remember any names of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I can't remember any names of the Jewish committee. No-- no names. You know, I have a memorial book here. But it's-- I will show it to you. It was published in New York. That was quite a few years ago. And it was published by Rabbi Wainderberg, I was talking about. He returned. He's a survivor. And he's still in New York. And he's a survivor. He came back. His wife was killed. And he had a-- a Jewish congregation. You know, a-- a big temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and he-- and in this book-- unfortunately, it is Hungarian and Hebrew. You know? In this book, he is giving the names of [? the ?] committee's members. So I have to look it up. But I-- I don't know-- I can't remember-- it was so long ago-- the names of them. But I have it in this memorial book. Were there other people besides from Cluj, in the ghetto? Any other refugees from other parts of the country at all, that were with you? Or were they all from Cluj, basically?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that might have been people from Cluj, in the area. You know? Because you know, there was the countryside. Might have been from there. I'm not aware that there were other people from other cities there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food situation like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food situation was terrible. Now, some people had the foresight-- we didn't-- that they packed food. You know? Kept some food packages. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother had some eggs and some-- some-- some bread. You know? And we got very little food. And we had some water. But it was so little, you know? That it was a problem when you had to wash your hair. I had long hair, up to knees. I had two thick-- I had-- I had two thick braids around my head. You know? And it was really hard conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't have warm water. I mean, it was-- it was really rough. We had very little food, very little food. Our starvation diet started, I think, in the ghettos. You know, that was a-- an introduction to the German concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there, all day long? What was a typical day like inside the ghetto, for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a typical-- you know-- you know how-- how we Jewish people are. There were some entrepreneurs. You know, interestingly enough, wherever you put the Jews, you know, there is some little business, tradings.

speaker: interviewer
text: You trade in this, you get this. Some people, I don't know what, they had something that the others didn't have. you know? And-- and-- so-- so by then-- then-- then there were, you know, some rabbis in the ghetto, you know, who tried to do something. you know? To-- We tried to give, kind of, strength to each other. You know? But it was not-- we were-- we were practically prisoners there. We couldn't get out of the ghetto. See? We were surrounded by guards. And [? that's why ?] [? we ?] [? were ?] [? there. ?] And the-- the worst part was, I think-- see I-- I-- that-- that we-- we didn't-- we didn't do anything. You know? We couldn't do much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't do much. There was nothing to do. It was a temporary waiting for something much better. And look what we got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any girlfriends inside the ghetto that you talked with at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were some girls who were from my city, you know. And I even had a schoolmate there. Yeah, some-- I had. And-- and your question is why didn't I have before-- I answer your, now, question-- more, maybe friends, you were asking me, and in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we-- we Jewish students, we stuck together. You see? Because we were-- we were persecuted, discriminated, and shunned. That's the truth. We were shunned from the others. So inside the ghetto, the friends that you had, were you able to get together and talk at all about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we could talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could-- that's one thing. Well, we were talking about the good times. You know? And-- and we were talking about hoping now, this is just [? provisorical. ?] And then the memories, and the-- you know, sharing thoughts. And some said, well, it's not going to be so bad, probably. Not going to last. And you know, I think that lots of-- of-- of the conversation was about the future and the hope for something better.
